entry.title: SIESTA on Paperscape
entry.content:
While preparing one other talk I've ran into a funny feature of PaperScape that allows to view a graph of references or citations of a certain paper on arXiv. Moreover, such graph can be referred as a link (as stated in this post).

So here is the citations' projection of the original SIESTA paper onto the arXiv manifold - the overall number of citations of that publication is one order of magnitude higher, spanning over 11,5+k citing papers in total!

And connected is one new star in this sub-Universe, referring to its massive predecessor: SIESTA: recent developments and applications. Their relations are visible on the - yet not very bright - references graph (mind the "heavy star" to the bottom right):

entry.title: A Hundred Bucks Second Best Spent
entry.content:
The best buy for a spare hundred bucks would be a second-hand bike, if you don't have any. Or, within an advanced cityscape, a one year subscription for rental bike service. But after that, what should a second hundred go for?

My answer would be, again, if you don't have anything like that yet - go get yourself a 3D printer. Not a professional designer's model. Limit yourself for a hundred bucks, try to get the best for that amount.

It won't be a quiet pleasure to use. It won't be able to create something complicated from the ready-to-use proprietary STL files.

It will, though, change the way you think.

My Tronxy X1 Kit arrived in two boxes, disassembled. That's how I learned what word "kit" meant in the title. Assembling it was fun, even more surprising fun was to see that it'd actually worked, with only minor artifacts of motion visible on large and curly shapes.

No heating bed, single nozzle, PLA only. Dimensions are also rather small: work space is 15cm cube. Not much to brag about, but...

It is enough to set a certain reassuring feeling:

Many of the things I'll ever need I just create on demand!

From keyboard casing and AC hoof hangers to numerous containers and organizers, plus some creative madness using procedural CAD and attempts to practice generative art.

In a sense, I think, the device has already paid off. Or will soon, after I replace it's cheap extruder.

Oh and for the third best spent, check the stores for earbuds with active noise cancelling. Some decent models already available for one hundred bucks. Amazing.

entry.title: Simple Vega-Lite plotter app script
entry.content:
Update [04/2021]

A couple of discoveries followed my initial playing with Clojure and Vega library.

First, the author of Babashka himself pointed to me on twitter that Bb is now bundled with http-kit which should streamline creation of app-server scripts. No need to bootstrap everything from scratch, unlike in the following example.

Another one is Feedgnuplot: a nice frontend to Gnuplot that simplifies data piping to charts. I ran into it while searching for an ascii-terminal plotter that would fit for my StumpWM extensions. However, it is in fact a handy "every day gnuplot inliner".

Babaska + Vega-lite plotter app (Original)

For the Clojure shell scripting environment with a funny name Babashka there is a simple example of a web server. It's funny and impressive at the same time, being only a short single script.

I've remixed it and thus got me a console Vega-Lite plotter (that of course requires a browser to view the plot).

The command looks like

$ vega-serv.bb -p PORT -f FILE

Where FILE is a clojure EDN with a map containing full Vega-Lite grammar spec for the plot. It is converted to JSON via Cheshire library shipped with Babashka. An example that contains data points:

{:$schema     "https://vega.github.io/schema/vega-lite/v4.json"
 :description "A simple bar chart with embedded data."
 :mark "bar"
 :encoding {:x {:field "a" :type "ordinal"}
            :y {:field "b" :type "quantitative"}}
 :width  800
 :height 600
 ;;
 :data {:values [{:a "A" :b 28}
                 {:a "B" :b 55}
                 {:a "C" :b 43}
                 {:a "D" :b 91}]}}

Looks even cleaner than JSON to me.

I might want to remix it a bit further, to e.g. serve a CSV-file with data points separately from the view-encoding map. But right now I see whole maps like this more likely to be built inside a shell pipe through other calls to BB.

Such implementation already has a couple of pros:

  1. Starts pretty fast, thanks to Babashka binary
  2. I can tweak the resulting plot by editing the description file, no need to stop the server
  3. It is a single-file exectutable script placed under $PATH

Motivation? A rapid cli- plotter better than gnuplot!

entry.title: File descriptors exhausted with `launch-program`
entry.content:
I've recently introduced an issue in my workflow when using Common Lisp's UIOP standard facility for launching asynchronous system calls. It appears to be quite a critical one since I'm planning to use that functionality a lot with my side-effect manager.

The following code will fail after a couple hundred (maybe thousand) of iterations, complaining about "too many open files":

  (loop
 :repeat 1000
 :do  (let ((proc (uiop:launch-program
                   "cat ~/.bashrc"
                   :output :stream :error-output :stream)))

        (print (uiop:process-info-pid proc)) ; show PID

        (uiop:slurp-stream-lines        ; collect output lines
         (uiop:process-info-output proc))

        (unless (= 0 (uiop:wait-process proc)) ; wait till end
          (print "Fail!"))))

Experimentally I found out that uiop:close-streams should be used to mitigate the bug. Which is in fact written in the documentation string, saying:

Needs to be run whenever streams were requested by passing :stream to :input, :output, or :error-output.

Which takes place since I do request those outputs as streams.

But how do I know about it in advance? Well, turns out that the corresponding chapter in the ASDF manual opens with description of close-streams, because alphabet. So it's one of the "RTFM" cases.

So this works, as many-many times I want:

 (loop
 :repeat 100000
 :do  (let ((proc (uiop:launch-program
                   "cat .bashrc"
                   :output :stream :error-output :stream)))

        (print (uiop:process-info-pid proc)) ; show PID

        (uiop:slurp-stream-lines        ; collect output lines
         (uiop:process-info-output proc))

        (unless (= 0 (uiop:wait-process proc)) ; wait till end
          (print "Fail!"))

        (uiop:close-streams proc))) ; <- DAT FIXES

And given that I defined a macro for this most common pattern of program launching, just not to forget to close-streams again. A raw anaphora will serve till I produce something more clever:

 (defmacro aproc (command &body body)
  "Anaphoric process launcher. Refer to process instance as PROC in &BODY."
  `(let ((proc (uiop:launch-program
                ,command :output :stream :error-output :stream)))
     (values ,@body (uiop:close-streams proc))))

entry.title: Visuals Made Sane
entry.content:
Almost every time I had to produce a visual representation for - literally - anything, it used to be disturbing. A balance between control, speed, accuracy and easiness seemed unreachable even for a routine plotting of data at hand. Well, not anymore!

A brief research lead me to a conclusion that the sanest way to draw something fast, efficient and nicely-looking these days is to use that very piece of software you are probably already running.

Yes, I mean the mighty web browser. Some observations follow.


Presentations: Reveal.js

Though I like the idea of the beamer LaTeX package of programmable presentation files, I hate the package itself for the tremendous amount of effort it requires to output a PDF that won't cause your audiences' eyes bleed. Yes, out-of-the-box beamer is plain ugly. Just fixing its looks would take days for an unprofessional layout designer.

Forget it. Use PowerPoint or LibreOffice Impress, or any other WYSIWYG editor. Everything goes for this ungrateful task, especially if you're short on time. But in case you don't have to rush and know a bit of markup, have a glance at reveal.js.

Under the hood it's the same idea of using hypertext instead of a visual editor for presentation outline. Yeah, like beamer, only that:

  • it looks way better from the start;
  • markup language is same as for the web;
  • it has all the key features of visual editors.

The last is true when reveal.js is served through npm, bringing even familiar speaker's screen with previews, notes and timer. Though this goes as unnecessary "extra". To build a presentation with reveal.js one really needs only a browser and text editor, meaning zero installation overhead.

With support for LaTeX formula notation built-in through MatJax, and simple but elegant block layouts available with use of modern css, I'd say that reveal.js is "more beamer than beamer".

Plots & Charts: Vega-Lite

Plotting was a cursed subject to me where no "golden point" tools seemed to be found. I wont' bother naming cons of gnuplot, matplotlib, plplot etc, each of them has a bunch. And the main problem is that with any of the popular plotters charting just simply takes a lot of time. Nothing handy seemed to appear outside of rather enclosed environments like Mathematica or Racket's plot library (one of the cool features of Racket, a language that is an ivory tower of its own). With standalone plotters it takes too much red tape to make a chart fine enough to be shared. Even with all the data at hand. It hurts.

All I want is an accurate, powerful, portable solution with concise and understandable semantic to draw them plots from raw data and from what is piped from my programs as well. Is it too much to ask in 2020? Well, in fact a good graphics grammar design is a non-trivial topic, with books and papers published.

The paper from the latter link describes the design of one such grammar system - Vega-Lite. I discovered it accidentally, but fell in love at once.

According to the paper:

Vega-Lite combines a traditional grammar of graphics, providing visual encoding rules and a composition algebra for layered and multi-view displays, with a novel grammar of interaction.

It allows to specify compositions of charts on a canvas, interactive selections and data transforms. Vega-Lite (coupled with low-level underlying Vega engine) can display quite sophisticated styles of charts (3-dimensional plots are on the go), requiring rather short specification from a user. I'd say, go read the design paper for details, then address the reference guide.

But first look at an example. Here it is:

And it's not just an export form, but the example itself. It's rendered using Js through Vega-Lite's modules, on this very page. Here's the complete code:

 if ('undefined' === typeof visVegaSpec) {
    var visVegaSpec = {
    "$schema": "https://vega.github.io/schema/vega-lite/v4.json",
    "description": "Vega-lite chart assembled with CL web libs.",
    "title": "Vega-lite example",
    "data": {
        "values": [
        {
            "step": 5,
            "flux": [
                0.09153984953435515,
                0.024628329219937537,
                0.25424937682847376
                ],
            "component": ["X", "Y", "Z"]
        },
        {
            "step": 4,
            "flux": [
                0.06789449311470507,
                0.02272393291011522,
                0.19036725133450748
                ],
            "component": ["X", "Y", "Z"]
        },
        {
            "step": 3,
            "flux": [
                0.046126591721860055,
                0.016882629311779522,
                0.1282773775209184
                ],
            "component": ["X", "Y", "Z"]
        },
        {
            "step": 2,
            "flux": [
                0.023685530610426934,
                0.08976102834966887,
                0.06519893303184349
                ],
            "component": ["X", "Y", "Z"]
        }
        ],
    },
    "mark": {
        "type": "line",
        "point": {"filled": false, "fill": "white"}
        },
    "encoding": {
        "x": {
        "field": "step",
        "type": "quantitative",
        "title": "step number",
        "axis": {"tickCount": 4}
        },
        "y": {
        "field": "flux",
        "type": "quantitative",
        "title": "component value",
        "axis": {"tickCount": 4}
        },
        "color": {
        "field": "component",
        "type": "nominal",
        "scale": {
            "domain": ["X", "Y", "Z"],
            "range": ["#dc143c", "#ff8c00", "#228b22"]
        }
        }
    },
    "config": {"axis": {"grid": true}},
    "transform": [{"flatten": ["component", "flux"]}],
    "width": 400, "height": 300
    };
  };
  vegaEmbed('#vis', visVegaSpec);

Note first that the whole plot spec is just a JSON. Here it includes the data points too, but in principle the data can be served from a separate csv-file. That would leave here only the plot view spec that is short, readable and in general makes sense.

Mind that round button in the right top corner for the chart export options. One of it says "Open in Vega Editor". It spawns an interactive editor in the browser for that very plot you are looking at.where the whole plot view can be edited live. Great to tackle with different plotting styles!

So, creating a plot as easy as editing a simple html-template with Vega-Lite spec. One can go further, serving a trivial single-page web app with the plot spec and data assembled and serialized as JSON (a task for which plenty of libs are available everywhere).

That app can be improved again a little bit, inspired by vega-lite with websockets example. That brings fully dynamic auto-updating charts! That's how I use Vega coupled with my computational workflows, having the plot updated after each step processed with our Quantum-Mechanical heavy artillery. One may think about live dashboards powered with only a Raspberry Pi running a browser. I can't stop thinking about them now. I do all the time...


In this test run the Heat Flux components from the two DFT programs get straight onto the plot right as each Molecular Dynamics step is processed.

3D scenes: WebGL

For everyone trying to enter low-level 3D rendering nullprogram's advice is to start directly with WebGL, since it utilizes the most modern standards and has very low overhead. Again, only a modern web browser is needed - no need to drag a GUI window library. Portability is also high. Android devices do render WebGL, hell even single-board computers do render!

The author of the best WebGL tutorial I've seen so far also wrote an utility library TWGL that helps to set up the scene components and start drawing faster (without hiding any low-level parts of the WebGL API).

This brings you GL, and fast, but what about interactiveness? Remember that this framework runs entirely in browser. The program that can use javascript to control the DOM of pages it shows. So with access to the javascript console one could rebuild in parts or in whole an appearance of a page, e.g. what is rendered on its <canvas> element.

This idea is shown in this demo (I mirror the video below), where the author sends instructions from the Common Lisp REPL directly into his browser as Js instructions. Lisp forms are translated into javascript code using Parenscript library and sent as messages through websocket to a client page. Though it's a universal trick to inject any code on a page from any other programming environment (not necessarily Lisp), applied to WebGL canvas it creates an effect of manipulating a 3D scene without reloading anything!

And that last part can give a spin to a various tasty hobby projects... But those ideas I'll keep for myself for now. Okay, just a hint one more last time.

entry.title: My RPi Cyberpunk Info-Deck Console
entry.content:
A while ago I used to own an HP PocketPC device. Yep, that pre-smartphone era handheld personal planner. It did not make me very productive though, on the contrary, but there was definitely something to it. An enthralling tactile sensation of hunting pixels on the screen with a stylus pen - that kind of resistive screen one has to hit hard - added to the fascination of surfing through teh personal knowledgebase consisting of FreeDict dictionaries, .chm-formatted textbooks and Wikipedia snapshots. Back then all this treasure could fit on a 32Gb SD-card.

I guess dat muscle memory, as much as anything, is the reason I obtained this monster.

The Chasis

This one (it goes by the hostname mimir for the reasons explained later) is not a DIY device. I wanted a capable working handheld more than I wanted to build one myself. The only physical addition I made was a 3D-printed lid that covers the battery section on the bottom.
(NOTE: need a similar one on the top)

Many commercial indie handhelds are built on top of Raspberry Pi ARM boards or similar and sold for $100-$400. But a board like that can do more than be just a host for a number of retro console emulators. The creative tweaking is already part of the game with growing DIY culture in both hardware and software. That ends up with GameShell, embracing open standards and fantasy consoles, being out of stock, while Playdate - which creators took a path of restrictions and misbehavior towards the indie dev community - did not launch that well at all.


RetroPie's AGS port in action

Myself I went along with GameHAT low-budget variant. Not only the whole kit (the HAT plus RPi-3 plus 18650 li-ion battery) costs less than a hundred bucks, it also has demonstrates obvious tweakability. Its joypad, buttons, speakers etc. are those ones you'll get in any "Arduino first steps" educational kit, so much for the repairs. The screen is connected via 1-to-1 HDMI adapter: plug a monitor instead, get a keyboard - and here you have an emergency desktop. After a funny configuration quest, of course.

The Port

GameHAT comes with pre-built RetroPie image with support for joystick/buttons (realized through a kernel module I failed to compile from, apparently, a very indie piece of code). RetroPie bundles a collection of RetroARCH's emulation libs together with some PC goodies called ports under a handy EmulationStation UI overlay. Among those ports, together with Duke3d and Adventure Game Studio, hides "Pixel desktop" that is basically LXDE for RPi. Its installation allows to switch to Desktop from EmulationStation (and back on logout).

One bit of frustration was found in the way that setup supports fantasy consoles, in particular TIC-80. Somehow EmulationStation fails to launch with support for OpenGL turned on explicitly with raspi-config, that's needed for TIC-80 to work. And though RetroArch does have a libretro library for TIC, the performance is approximately two times slower than normal! A workaround is to launch desktop separately and build TIC from source.

Luckily the joystick is recognized by SDL and mapping software (remember, it talks straight to the linux kernel). In the desktop I use QJoyPad to map joystick to mouse, as well as give buttons the default clicking | scrolling | entering | cancelling roles:

Another input program that autostarts is the Onboard virtual keyboard. Fits well, and has a feature of "sticking" to fullscreen windows.


Onboard comes in handy when there is no remote keyboard at hand

And after those little tweaks it's time for real enhancement, where my Semantic Space tears through the fabric of reality with help of Emacs OS!

The Info-Deck


Elfeed-search contents screen

Work, emails, numerous chats and social networks do not leave neither time nor stamina for relaxing glancing through them news feeds. Elfeed emacs feedreader package on Mimir brings me back my rss subscriptions in places where there are no other distractions from lazy news consumption: in the canteen, while travelling or before getting asleep etc. Yeah, pun intended.

Elfeed's entry view

Important articles are tagged for analysis later on. I sync Elfeed's database with my private cloud through Syncthing, along with a set of Org-mode files that form my personal knowledgebase.

Speaking of latter, I was quite happy to find org-brain - a project that helps me turning my orgmode linear journals into a wiki-style mind maps, and brings no editing overhead. On the contrary, writing structural notes is actually faster in org-brain than in linear form. Perfect! Org-brain already consumed my Elfeed's subscriptions list and org-ref notes, and I just hope it won't get slow after the indexed knowledgebase grows tenfold.

Concept map view in Org-brain

With Mimir the heavy assets are not synced, only the wiki itself. And wandering through it sometimes does reveal me the overlooked connections... Btw, I had to build emacs v.26 for org-brain to work.

I estimate 2/3 of my time with Mimir I spend on thinking over feeds and thoughts. That justifies the hostname of this little thingie, reciting me unrevealed knowledge and counsel.

The End

Yes of course, games, we have some:

Though, honestly, quite often the old-school goodies often turn out to be enormously repetitive and boring. Yet some of them I manage to finish eventually...


Nope, not a hundred, not even close

entry.title: An Ode to Emacs
entry.content:
A rare positive result of the lockdown - increased friendship towards my editor of choice. After explaining configuration basics to a friend another day I think I can finally formulate what clings me to this ancient everlasting piece of software.

Code

Well, yeah, first reason: code developing tool. Major modes for almost everything that deserves a title of programming / scripting / markup language out there. But not only.

Take Language Server Protocol for introspection where you did not expect any. Projectile and magit to manage the state of the project. Even in vanilla emacs there are handy tools for development, including local variables and version control (useful for versioning systems other than git).

Then, amidst the lockdown madness, find out that all of this works on remote machines too through TRAMP. Yes, the same precious editing environment that took you so much effort to assemble. Awesome, isn't it?

Compose

Skipping the obvious (autocompletions, spellchecking, etc): Org-mode. Now a built-in part of vanilla Emacs, it's the greatest get-things-not-forgotten tool. It powers my private log, work and accounting notes and literally any documentation set that I compose.

It does not power this static blog for reasons I cannot well explain. Somehow I thought that tweaking a different static site generator would be faster than making Org export look fine and not at all crappy. I still do think so by the way.[1]

One distinct remarkable feature of org-mode is a support for fashy literate programming in the most literate way. Not only does org-babel have a big number of supported languages for source blocks - one can also join them in pipes.[2] Even those that need compilation! I one can feed an output of a C-block as an output to a Fortran-block within a scope of one document. This is possible because the medium here is the contents of the document itself, not the state of underlying interpreter "kernel".[3]

And of course for a researcher it is hard to find better citation system than John Kitchin's org-ref. It allows one to build her own collection of papers that is visible in org-mode buffers for note-taking and draft-editing. Charming!

Communicate

Mu4e for emails, telega.el for Telegram. Those cover the majority of the daily communications I do at the moment.

Most of other media accounts can be accessed through bitlbee + erc (Emacs built-in IRC client). The list is impressive, including Skype, Fb, Discord and Steam. Ideal to keep all this social rubbish in one place for chatting over weekends.

For those old-fashioned like me who still manage their lists of RSS subscriptions, good news: no need to stop, since there is elfeed and elfeed-org.[4] By the way, its author Chris Wellons writes an interesting blog about emacs' internals and other curious stuff. A good candidate for your refreshed feedlist.

Dark secrets revealed

If you are looking for the "dark secret of emacs", I can name a couple.

The first one lies in the prodigious extensiveness of the editor by design, which, over time, led to a series of really good plugins that have a great level of synergy among themselves. I list some examples above. Together they aid me with three daily activities: Coding, Composing and Communicating - within one universal editor.

Why does it matter? The answer is, of course, all of these tasks deal with manipulation of textual data. And a second glance on it reveals that emacs to date is maybe the best textual UI tool. Some push this idea to extreme limits, somewhat materializing the famous joke about an "OS without a proper editor".

I am not THAT zealous, but myself I find useful unifying the text-processing tasks. Being able to store an IM conversation branch as a diary entry, email the bibtex citations to colleagues and blog about all this without leaving an editor's window may sound like an overkill, but after a while it feels quite essential. It's all text after all!

Emacs has it's flaws and legacy anchors. (My biggest personal regret is that it still uses a somewhat clumsy emacs lisp language with some Common Lisp -like features instead of normal support for CL-standard). Yes, emacs is not perfect, let's say it is just very good. Or "the worst editor out there, except for the rest ones", if you prefer.

[1] Well, in fact I intentionally searched for (and found!) a Common Lisp static blogging platform package, because StumpWM. But that is another story for another time, maybe...

[2] John Kitchin showed another method, with tangling and linking through a makefile.

[3] Though session can often be specified where it makes sense.

[4] Myself I keep a setup of emacs+elfeed on my GameHat handheld, "showcased" in the welcome post XD. That guy also deserves a story.

entry.title: An Interactive Haven
entry.content:
Recently I took part in a discussion organized by CECAM[1] on the topic of Digital Learning, a subject which all of a sudden became critical to all the educational institutions. Of course there was a section on adaptation of existing courseware to remote learning formats. With opinions on difficulties to follow, grade and adapt to e-learning. And a traditional certain skepticism expressed on technologies that fail to create revolutions in education.

I agree on that, it's hard to imagine a revolt in internal thinking process by mere upgrade in the educational media. But certain breakthroughs can be achieved with modern technologies. False. They are already present, with help of tech that is decades old.

J.L.Borges pictured us books as labyrinths of symbols. He dreamed of libraries as an infinite combinatorial symbolic spaces, where all texts of natural languages are present - even those yet to be written. One can develop artistic writing skills by navigating and extending such a labyrinth - still incomplete yet already enormously large. Guides help to speed up learning, teaching helps to faster build a decent taste, but the labyrinth of fiction is self-contained, and lacks dreadful "motivation problem" if the goal also is placed within the walls, in a distant but observable section. A laboratory rat[2] that senses cheese learns to find it faster every next time.

Programming allows us to build extensible interactive environments for symbolic formalisms. Maxima and Wolfram Mathematica are both great examples, it's hard to find any better for illustration purposes. But there are more. The infamous SICP book has a younger sister also authored by G.J. Sussman. It is backed up with Scheme language environment[3] that implements core Classical Mechanics formalism and allows readers to simulate described examples - or their own. A popular online course NAND2Tetris ships a Verilog-like simplified HDL and its testing grounds in order to let students describe electronic components and simulate bigger systems using these custom gates.

These examples do not embody something breaking new neither in educational principles not in the underlying implementations (those have been developed a while ago as computer algebra systems). However, they demonstrate live reconstruction of labyrinths from Borges's dreams. One should just realize that building such an interactive haven, as much as exploring its dark waters, is tough. But Hell, how rewarding can it be!

[1] As the title suggests, they organize many events around Atomic & Molecular Simulations

[2] In no way an offensive comparison. Rats are adorable and smart. I used to own a pet rat a while ago. The biggest disenchantment is their short lifespan.

[3] Outside MIT the most suitable port seems to be the one I'm tinkering with now, based on Guile Scheme.

entry.title: Developing with Spack
entry.content:
The man-made Hell of dependencies management in HPC software has an efficient way out: Spack, initially established at Lawrence Livermore and actively community-supported manager of program packages and libraries for supercomputing. It's like e.g. Portage for Gentoo, but system-agnostic, and does not pollute user's environment since packages are linked through RPATH at compile-time instead of through dynamic (re)setting of environment variables at runtime (as is pefrormed by hierarchical module systems like lmod). This allows for the impressive feature list explained in a well-written documentation, making Spack a very useful cluster management tool.

But not only. A careful reader will be happy to see in it a killer app for actual code development in the chaos vortex of academic & research software.

Custom Repositories

First of all, managing dependencies, even secret ones, for a project in development is straightforward with Spack since it allows to have extra package repositories that shadow the default builtin package repo. This custom repository structure is same as default's: one package.py file per package with installation instructions, plus optional .patch-files alongside.

For composing package files the developers introduce what looks like handy on-top-of-python-DSL. Creation of a Spack script for conventionally well-written software package with e.g. CMake or Autotools is easy, but even archaic regex-processing of makefiles is also doable within Spack definitions. Installation phases like configure, build, install are explicitly marked (more on their roles later).

Given that, what project maintainers might want to have are:

  1. a fork of Spack's official git repo with changes meant to be pushed upstream for the benefit of the research society;
  2. small private repositories that replicate the structure of packages with experimental features not yet to be publicly available.

The dev-build command

One way to improve the documentation of Spack would be to introduce its dev-build command to the reader as soon as possible. Because turns out that it is THE ultimate command for package writing.

spack dev-build ... should be called from the downloaded software's codebase root. First, it allows for incremental build attempts up to a certain installation phase with --until= flag. This allows to test your new package file gradually, phase after phase, with less garbage and in a better way than spack install --keep-stage.

Second, all the dependencies for the package would be valid Spack dependencies.

Finally, it allows installation of local codebases under fictional version names, that might not be even listed under version directives in the package!

The developer workflow in the docs is in my opinion misleading. It is focused on the use of CMake setup that can bypass the lack of software build environment (remember one of the key features of Spack, allowing conflicting builds under one roof?). This, however, makes little sense if one is using an isolated compilation command, for example, in the text editor of choice.

Spack integrates module activation support and can load spec dependencies' modules as well with -r/--dependencies flag. For my emacs + projectile the .dir-locals file for recompilarion of e.g. Siesta binary looks like this:

((nil
  (projectile-project-compilation-dir . "Obj/")
  (projectile-project-compilation-cmd
   .
   "spack load gcc && spack load mpi &&
    spack load -r siesta@fake && make ")))

Only compiler and mpi-metapackage need to be load explicitly, the rest of the deps are activated via single spack load -r siesta@fake directive, where "fake" would be a fictional version name for dev-build command. And this becomes a standard template I use for all software that is maintained with help of Spack.

Spack for Siesta project

At the moment of writing I have a fork with fixed Atom pseudopotential-files generator and Siesta (LibGridXC + PSML support enabeled) in the siesta-dft branch of my fork of Spack. In case it proves useful on our clusters, these updates will be polished and pull-requested rather soon. Otherwise I will continue editing them on my own and make a pull request when ready.

entry.title: Fortls: comfy indexing of Fortran
entry.content:
If you happen to find yourself in HPC research or engineering, you will most probably deal with Fortran codebases at some point. With two major problems that hurt lazy young men's feelings in 2020: 1st being the ambiguity of which instructions from the standard form the desired subset of a useful Fortran number-crunching DSL, and the 2nd is (was) lack of introspection for a (modern) IDE.

While the solution of the first problem is found merely in personal experience and Sacred Wisdom of the Elders (never saw a book on Fortran that did not look like a flat dry reference index), solution for the second is open to the public: Chris Hansen's Fortran Language Server.

Of course, indexing tools for C and Fortran had existed for years before the Language Server Protocol was developed (and then grew up in popularity and implementations' number). But I have to admit that I never could make any of the *tags work anywhere near properly for projects as complex as Siesta. Not even complaining of their static nature and the need of some automation of index-building mechanisms: I wasn't even there.

On the other hand, Fortls is able to navigate through roughly 95% of definitions in the code of Siesta, missing only overloaded procs and, of course, code hidden in metaprogramming templates. For every symbol well seen, it allows to jump to the place where it's defined, list and rename all the occurrences throughout the project. Even more: it parses the Doxygen/FORD docstring-like comments and shows them in the hint-box! Plus support for subroutines' signature hinting and autocomplete.

All this might not sound very sexy today, especially if the language in question provided some introspection tools of its own, but mind that it's Fortran we are speaking about! Here, after decades of heavy science, monstrosities of hundreds of modules do exist with nothing more handy than grep to navigate'em prior to LSP.

The installation and configuration of Fortls is straightforward: it is a python package providing a command line tool that should be seen to an LSP-supporting editor. I actually spent more time tweaking the lsp-mode UI in emacs than making it work with fortls. According to my minimalistic taste I do not use signature popups, only the hinting UI-doc window (and it includes signatures anyway, together with types of variables under cursor). Autocomplete seems to work with company-mode out of the box.

On the project page Chris shows some screencasts of how working with Fortran Language Server looks in the Atom editor; here I briefly show the looks of my own setup in Emacs (mind that automatic update of a symbol's description):


Fortls + emacs lsp-mode: hover, reference finder and jump-to-def.
